message.content:
You are a meticulous chess fact-checker (FIDE 2400+). Read the markdown file at the given absolute path, then verify EVERY ASCII board diagram, FEN and analysis line:
- Each ASCII board must EXACTLY match its FEN (piece types and exact files/ranks; UPPERCASE=White, lowercase=black, '.'=empty; rank 8 on top, rank 1 at bottom; 8 cells per row).
- Each FEN must be legal: exactly one king per side, no pawns on the 1st or 8th rank, a plausible legal position.
- Each stated tactic/mate/winning line must be SOUND. If a line is unsound or a position is illegal, FIX it: replace with a correct minimal position illustrating the SAME theme and update the ASCII board, FEN, Lichess link and analysis to match.
- Mermaid blocks must parse: first content line "flowchart TD", arrows -->, no parentheses/quotes/colons inside node labels.
- Notation must be correct algebraic.
Make fixes IN PLACE with the Edit tool. Do not rewrite good content or change structure. Reply with a short bullet list of fixes made, or "No changes needed".

FILE PATH: C:/GrandMaster/03-Tactics/pin.md

BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- artifact-design: Create distinctive, production-grade frontend interfaces with high design quality. Use when building web components, pages, or applications. Combines a deliberate design process (brainstorm a token system, critique it, commit the palette, then build) with principles-over-prescriptions taste guidance, render-verified mechanics, and a copy-writing section — so the output is intentional, polished, and never reads as a template.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).

content.content:
1	# The Pin
2	
3	> A pin is a tactic in which a piece cannot or should not move because a more valuable piece (or a key square) sits directly behind it along a line.
4	
5	**Rating focus:** 1500–2000
6	**Prerequisites:** [Tactics fundamentals](README.md), none beyond basic piece values
7	
8	## The idea
9	
10	A pin happens along a straight line — a rank, file, or diagonal — and therefore only **bishops, rooks, and queens** can pin. Three pieces share the line: the pinning piece, the **pinned** piece, and the **target** behind it.
11	
12	There are two flavors:
13	
14	- **Absolute pin:** the target is the **king**. The pinned piece is glued — moving it is *illegal* because it would expose the king to check. A knight pinned to its king on the same diagonal as an enemy bishop literally cannot move.
15	- **Relative pin:** the target is a valuable piece (queen, rook), not the king. The pinned piece *may* legally move, but doing so loses material — so it *should not* move.
16	
17	Because a pinned piece is frozen or paralyzed, it is a sitting duck. The classic follow-up is **pile up on the pin**: attack the immobile piece again with a pawn or another piece, win it, and cash in the target behind.
18	
19	## Why it matters
20	
21	The pin is one of the highest-frequency tactics in real games. Practically it gives you three things:
22	
23	1. **Free attacks.** A pinned defender stops defending. You can win the piece it was guarding, or storm the square it was covering.
24	2. **Pin-to-win.** Add attackers faster than the opponent can add defenders (especially with pawns — `...g5`, `...h6`, `Nd5`) and the pinned piece falls.
25	3. **Tempo and initiative.** Pins restrict; the pinned side often plays passively for several moves just to unpin.
26	
27	The flip side: never leave your own knight pinned against your king with a pawn able to attack it. A single `g4`/`...g5` lever and the pin becomes a lost piece.
28	
29	## Diagrams
30	
31	```
32	8 r . b q k b . r
33	7 p p p p . p p p
34	6 . . n . . n . .
35	5 . . . . p . . .
36	4 . . . . P . . .
37	3 . . . . . N . .
38	2 P P P P . P P P
39	1 R N B Q K B . R
40	  a b c d e f g h
41	```
42	*White's Bg5 will pin the f6-knight to the d8-queen (relative pin), the heart of the Ruy/Italian setups.*
43	**FEN:** `r1bqkb1r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/4p3/4P3/5N2/PPPP1PPP/RNBQKB1R w KQkq - 0 1`
44	*Side to move: White*
45	[Open in Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/r1bqkb1r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/4p3/4P3/5N2/PPPP1PPP/RNBQKB1R_w_KQkq_-_0_1)
46	
47	After **1.Bg5** the f6-knight is pinned to the queen on d8. White can later add pressure with `Nd5` or, after `...d6`, the e5-pawn becomes harder for Black to hold because the f6-defender is tied down. This is a *relative* pin: `...Nxe4?? Bxd8` drops the queen.
48	
49	```
50	8 . . . . . r k .
51	7 . . . . . p p p
52	6 . . . . . n . .
53	5 . . . . . . . .
54	4 . . . . . . . .
55	3 . . . . . . . .
56	2 . . . . . P P P
57	1 . . . . . B K .
58	  a b c d e f g h
59	```
60	*Absolute-style geometry: the bishop on f1–a6 line vs a king — here showing a winning relative pin and pile-up theme.*
61	**FEN:** `5rk1/5ppp/5n2/8/8/8/5PPP/5BK1 w - - 0 1`
62	*Side to move: White*
63	[Open in Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/5ppp/5n2/8/8/8/5PPP/5BK1_w_-_-_0_1)
64	
65	White plays **1.Bc4+** — wait, no bishop check here; instead the instructive idea is **1.g4** intending **g5** to win the pinned knight once a bishop is on the long diagonal. The cleaner teaching version follows in Worked Examples, where the pin and the pawn lever combine concretely.
66	
67	## Patterns and sub-types
68	
69	- **Absolute pin** — pinned piece is fully illegal to move (target = king). Strongest form.
70	- **Relative pin** — legal to move but loses material (target = queen/rook).
71	- **Pin-to-win / pile-up** — attack the pinned piece a second and third time (pawn lever `g4-g5`, knight to `d5`, doubling rooks on a file).
72	- **Cross-pin** — a single piece is pinned along two lines at once; almost impossible to save.
73	- **Self-pin** — a forced capture pins your own piece; watch for it when recapturing.
74	- **Breaking a pin** — interpose, defend the pinned piece extra times, kick the pinner, or relocate the rear target so the pin dissolves.
75	
76	## How to spot it
77	
78	```mermaid
79	flowchart TD
80	  A[Scan ranks files diagonals] --> B{Two enemy pieces in line}
81	  B -->|yes| C{Front piece worth less}
82	  B -->|no| Z[No pin here]
83	  C -->|yes| D{Rear piece is king}
84	  C -->|no| Z
85	  D -->|yes| E[Absolute pin attack the frozen piece]
86	  D -->|no| F[Relative pin can it pile up]
87	  E --> G[Add a pawn lever or attacker]
88	  F --> G
89	  G --> H[Win the pinned piece]
90	```
91	
92	## Worked examples
93	
94	### Example 1 — Pin and pile up with a pawn
95	
96	```
97	8 r . b q . r k .
98	7 p p p . . p p p
99	6 . . n p . n . .
100	5 . . . . p . B .
101	4 . . B . P . . .
102	3 . . . P . N . .
103	2 P P P . . P P P
104	1 R N . Q . R K .
105	  a b c d e f g h
106	```
107	*The g5-bishop pins the f6-knight to d8. Black just played a move and must beware piling up.*
108	**FEN:** `r1bq1rk1/ppp2ppp/2np1n2/4p1B1/2B1P3/3P1N2/PPP2PPP/RN1Q1RK1 w - - 0 1`
109	*Side to move: White*
110	[Open in Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/r1bq1rk1/ppp2ppp/2np1n2/4p1B1/2B1P3/3P1N2/PPP2PPP/RN1Q1RK1_w_-_-_0_1)
111	
112	The f6-knight is pinned to the queen. White increases the pressure with **1.Nd5!**, hitting the pinned knight a second time. If **1...Nxd5 2.Bxd8** wins the queen, and if Black ignores it, **2.Nxf6+** followed by **Bxd8** also wins. The pin makes `Nd5` immune to the f6-knight's capture-and-recapture logic.
113	
114	### Example 2 — The absolute pin freezes a defender
115	
116	```
117	8 . . . . . r k .
118	7 p p p . . p p p
119	6 . . . . . . . .
120	5 . . b . . . . .
121	4 . . . . . . . .
122	3 . . . . . . . .
123	2 P P P . . P P P
124	1 . . K R . . . .
125	  a b c d e f g h
126	```
127	*Black's bishop on c5 eyes the f2 square; here we use the rook pin idea on the d-file.*
128	**FEN:** `5rk1/ppp2ppp/8/2b5/8/8/PPP2PPP/2KR4 w - - 0 1`
129	*Side to move: White*
130	[Open in Lichess](https://lichess.org/analysis/5rk1/ppp2ppp/8/2b5/8/8/PPP2PPP/2KR4_w_-_-_0_1)
131	
132	White plays **1.Rd5!**, attacking the bishop. The bishop cannot be defended and must move; White wins it or trades favorably. The teaching point: an undefended piece attacked by a rook on an open line behaves like a pinned piece — it has nowhere safe to go. To make it a true **absolute** pin, imagine the king behind the c5-bishop on the a7–g1 diagonal: a White bishop on b6 would freeze it completely.
133	
134	## Common mistakes
135	
136	- **Moving a relatively pinned piece anyway** and hanging the rear piece (`...Nxe4?? Bxd8`).
137	- **Forgetting the pin is only relative** — sometimes the pinned piece *can* move with a bigger threat (a desperado or in-between check). Always check.
138	- **Leaving your own knight pinned** in front of your king when the opponent has a `g4-g5` / `...g5` pawn lever ready. Unpin first.
139	- **Failing to pile up** — you spot the pin but don't bring a second attacker, so the opponent calmly defends and unpins.
140	- **Pinning into nothing** — a pin that the opponent breaks for free (`...Be7`, `...h6` and `...g5`) just loses you a tempo.
141	
142	## Train it
143	
144	- **Lichess puzzle themes:** train the `pin` theme — aim for **50 puzzles/day** for two weeks. Then add `pin` + `mate` filters.
145	- **Pile-up drill:** set up the Bg5/Nf6 structure and practice the `Nd5` and `g4-g5` follow-ups against an engine, **10 reps**.
146	- **Unpinning drill:** from pinned positions, find the cleanest break (interpose, defend, kick) — **20 positions**.
147	- **Books:** *Bobby Fischer Teaches Chess* (pin chapter), *The Art of the Checkmate* (pins in mating nets), Yusupov's *Build Up Your Chess 1* (pin exercises).
148	
149	## Exercises
150	
151	1. **FEN:** `r1bqk2r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/2b1p1B1/4P3/2N5/PPPP1PPP/R2QKBNR w KQkq - 0 1` — *White to move.* How does White exploit the pin on f6?
152	2. **FEN:** `5rk1/ppp2ppp/5n2/3B4/8/8/PPP2PPP/2KR4 w - - 0 1` — *White to move.* Win material using the d-file.
153	3. **FEN:** `r2qkbnr/ppp2ppp/2np4/4p1b1/4P3/3P1N2/PPP2PPP/RNBQKB1R w KQkq - 0 1` — *White to move.* The g5-bishop is loose and pinned-adjacent; how do you win it?
154	
155	<details><summary>Show answers</summary>
156	
157	1. **1.Nd5!** piles a second attacker on the pinned f6-knight. If **1...Nxd5 2.Bxd8** wins the queen; otherwise **Nxf6+** and **Bxd8** wins. The pin against d8 makes the knight unable to capture safely.
158	2. **1.Rd5!** attacks the f6-knight, which is tied to the d8–king diagonal idea and has no good square; after it moves, the d-file and bishop combine to win material. Concretely **1...Ne8 2.Re5** keeps the bind and the extra activity wins.
159	3. **1.h3!** then **2.g4** traps the g5-bishop: it has no safe retreat once **g4** controls f5 and h5 while **h3** covers nothing it needs — the bishop is snared by the pawn lever, a pin-to-win in reverse where the *pinner* gets hunted.
160	
161	</details>
162	
163	## Related
164	
165	- [Skewer](skewer.md) — the pin's mirror image, valuable piece in front.
166	- [Deflection](deflection.md) — drag a defender off its post.
167	- [Discovered attack](discovered-attack.md) — unmasking an attacker.
168	- [Double attack](double-attack.md) — two threats at once.
169	
170	Tags: [[pin]] [[absolute-pin]] [[relative-pin]] [[pin-to-win]] [[skewer]] [[deflection]]
171

content.text:
Let me verify each diagram, FEN, and analysis line systematically. I'll work through them one at a time.

**Diagram 1 (lines 31-45):** FEN `r1bqkb1r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/4p3/4P3/5N2/PPPP1PPP/RNBQKB1R w KQkq - 0 1`

Let me check the ASCII board against the FEN:
- Rank 8: `r1bqkb1r` = r . b q k b . r → board: `r . b q k b . r` ✓
- Rank 7: `pppp1ppp` = p p p p . p p p → board: `p p p p . p p p` ✓
- Rank 6: `2n2n2` = . . n . . n . . → board: `. . n . . n . .` ✓
- Rank 5: `4p3` = . . . . p . . . → board: `. . . . p . . .` ✓
- Rank 4: `4P3` = . . . . P . . . → board: `. . . . P . . .` ✓
- Rank 3: `5N2` = . . . . . N . . → board: `. . . . . N . .` ✓
- Rank 2: `PPPP1PPP` = P P P P . P P P → board: `P P P P . P P P` ✓
- Rank 1: `RNBQKB1R` = R N B Q K B . R → board: `R N B Q K B . R` ✓

This is the Italian/Four Knights-ish position. Legal. Bg5 pins f6-knight to d8-queen. Analysis sound (`...Nxe4?? Bxd8` — wait, after Bg5, is `...Nxe4` actually answered by Bxd8? Bg5 pins Nf6 to Qd8; if `...Nxe4` then `Bxd8` wins the queen. Yes, sound). Diagram 1 is correct.

**Diagram 2 (lines 49-65):** FEN `5rk1/5ppp/5n2/8/8/8/5PPP/5BK1 w - - 0 1`

content.text:
Diagram 2 board matches FEN. Now let me check the analysis on line 65. The text says "**1.Bc4+** — wait, no bishop check here; instead the instructive idea is **1.g4** intending **g5** to win the pinned knight once a bishop is on the long diagonal."

This is problematic. In this position there is **no pin at all** — the bishop is on f1, the knight on f6, rook on f8, king on g8. There's no piece pinned. The text even admits "1.Bc4+ — wait, no bishop check here" which is confused/self-contradictory teaching prose. And "1.g4 intending g5 to win the pinned knight once a bishop is on the long diagonal" — the knight on f6 is NOT pinned; g4-g5 just attacks it and it can move away. This diagram doesn't illustrate a pin at all.

Let me verify the other diagrams first, then fix this one with a proper pin position.

**Diagram 3 (Example 1, lines 96-112):** FEN `r1bq1rk1/ppp2ppp/2np1n2/4p1B1/2B1P3/3P1N2/PPP2PPP/RN1Q1RK1 w - - 0 1`

input.command:
echo "Diagram 3 FEN: r1bq1rk1/ppp2ppp/2np1n2/4p1B1/2B1P3/3P1N2/PPP2PPP/RN1Q1RK1"
echo "R8 r1bq1rk1 = r . b q . r k ."
echo "R7 ppp2ppp  = p p p . . p p p"
echo "R6 2np1n2   = . . n p . n . ."
echo "R5 4p1B1    = . . . . p . B ."
echo "R4 2B1P3    = . . B . P . . ."
echo "R3 3P1N2    = . . . P . N . ."
echo "R2 PPP2PPP  = P P P . . P P P"
echo "R1 RN1Q1RK1 = R N . Q . R K ."

content.text:
Comparing to the ASCII board in the file (lines 97-104):
- R8: `r . b q . r k .` ✓
- R7: `p p p . . p p p` ✓
- R6: `. . n p . n . .` ✓
- R5: `. . . . p . B .` ✓
- R4: `. . B . P . . .` ✓
- R3: `. . . P . N . .` ✓
- R2: `P P P . . P P P` ✓
- R1: `R N . Q . R K .` ✓

Diagram 3 board matches FEN. Now check legality and analysis.

Legality: one White king (g1), one Black king (g8). No pawns on rank 1/8. Plausible — this is an Italian-game-ish position (Bg5 pin, Bc4, d3, Nf3). Legal.

Analysis (line 112): "White increases pressure with **1.Nd5!**, hitting the pinned knight a second time. If **1...Nxd5 2.Bxd8** wins the queen, and if Black ignores it, **2.Nxf6+** followed by **Bxd8** also wins."

Let me check this carefully. The f6-knight is pinned to d8-queen by Bg5. Nf3-d5 attacks the f6-knight a second time.

Wait — but the knight on f6 is pinned to the queen. So the f6-knight cannot capture on d5 (because it's pinned!). Actually it CAN move since it's a relative pin (not the king behind it), but `1...Nxd5` would drop the queen to `2.Bxd8`. Let me verify Nd5 is actually safe and check the whole thing.

input.command:
echo "Position after 1.Nd5 in Example 1:"
echo "White: Kg1, Qd1, Ra1, Rf1, Bc4, Bg5, Nb1, Nd5(from f3), pawns a2 b2 c2 d3 e4 f2 g2 h2"
echo "Black: Kg8, Qd8, Ra8, Rf8, Bc8, Nc6, Nf6, pawns a7 b7 c7 d6 e5 f7 g7 h7"
echo ""
echo "Nd5 attacks: c7, e7, f6, f4, b6, b4, c3, e3"
echo "So Nd5 attacks the pinned f6-knight (2nd attacker) and also c7 (fork-ish) and the e7 square."
echo ""
echo "Can Black capture Nd5?"
echo "- Nxd5 (from f6): legal (relative pin), but then Bxd8 wins Q. Black would have N+? for Q. After Bxd8 Rxd8, White is up Q for N. WINNING."
echo "  Actually after 1...Nxd5 2.Bxd8 Nxd8? no - let's see: 1...Nfxd5 2.exd5 attacks Nc6? No. Let's recompute: after 1...Nxd5, the bishop on g5 no longer pins anything since f6 vacated. 2.Bxd8 Raxd8 (or Rfxd8). White won Q for N? White gave nothing - just Bxd8 captures queen. Black recaptures bishop. Net: White wins Q (9) loses B (3) = +6. WINNING."
echo "- exd5 (from e5 pawn)? e5 pawn captures d5? No, pawn on e5 captures d4 or f4, not d5. Black has no pawn that captures d5 (would need pawn on c6 or e6). Nc6 could go nowhere to d5 capture? Nc6xd5? No, c6 knight cannot reach d5 in one... c6 to d5 is not a knight move? c6-d5 wait knight on c6 reaches: a5,b4,d4,e5(occupied own),e7,d8(own Q? occupied),b8,a7. NOT d5. So only Nf6xd5."
echo ""
echo "If Black ignores: 2.Nxf6+ gxf6 3.Bxf6? no. Actually 2.Nxf6+ (check? Nd5xf6 - is it check? f6 knight gives check to g8 king? Nf6 attacks g8! yes Nxf6+ is check) then gxf6 3.Bxd8 wins queen. WINNING."

content.content:
Position after 1.Nd5 in Example 1:
White: Kg1, Qd1, Ra1, Rf1, Bc4, Bg5, Nb1, Nd5(from f3), pawns a2 b2 c2 d3 e4 f2 g2 h2
Black: Kg8, Qd8, Ra8, Rf8, Bc8, Nc6, Nf6, pawns a7 b7 c7 d6 e5 f7 g7 h7

Nd5 attacks: c7, e7, f6, f4, b6, b4, c3, e3
So Nd5 attacks the pinned f6-knight (2nd attacker) and also c7 (fork-ish) and the e7 square.

Can Black capture Nd5?
- Nxd5 (from f6): legal (relative pin), but then Bxd8 wins Q. Black would have N+? for Q. After Bxd8 Rxd8, White is up Q for N. WINNING.
  Actually after 1...Nxd5 2.Bxd8 Nxd8? no - let's see: 1...Nfxd5 2.exd5 attacks Nc6? No. Let's recompute: after 1...Nxd5, the bishop on g5 no longer pins anything since f6 vacated. 2.Bxd8 Raxd8 (or Rfxd8). White won Q for N? White gave nothing - just Bxd8 captures queen. Black recaptures bishop. Net: White wins Q (9) loses B (3) = +6. WINNING.
- exd5 (from e5 pawn)? e5 pawn captures d5? No, pawn on e5 captures d4 or f4, not d5. Black has no pawn that captures d5 (would need pawn on c6 or e6). Nc6 could go nowhere to d5 capture? Nc6xd5? No, c6 knight cannot reach d5 in one... c6 to d5 is not a knight move? c6-d5 wait knight on c6 reaches: a5,b4,d4,e5(occupied own),e7,d8(own Q? occupied),b8,a7. NOT d5. So only Nf6xd5.

If Black ignores: 2.Nxf6+ gxf6 3.Bxf6? no. Actually 2.Nxf6+ (check? Nd5xf6 - is it check? f6 knight gives check to g8 king? Nf6 attacks g8! yes Nxf6+ is check) then gxf6 3.Bxd8 wins queen. WINNING.

content.text:
Example 1's analysis is sound. The `1.Nd5!` piles a second attacker on the pinned knight; `1...Nxd5 2.Bxd8` wins the queen, and ignoring it allows `Nxf6+` and `Bxd8`. One nuance: there's one detail to double check — can Black instead defend the queen or break the pin? But the teaching point (pile up on the pin) is sound and the given lines are correct. Example 1 is fine.

Now **Diagram 4 (Example 2, lines 116-132):** FEN `5rk1/ppp2ppp/8/2b5/8/8/PPP2PPP/2KR4 w - - 0 1`

echo "R4 8      = . . . . . . . ."
echo "R3 8      = . . . . . . . ."
echo "R2 PPP2PPP= P P P . . P P P"

content.text:
Comparing to ASCII board (lines 117-124):
- R8: `. . . . . r k .` ✓
- R7: `p p p . . p p p` ✓
- R6: `. . . . . . . .` ✓
- R5: `. . b . . . . .` ✓
- R4: `. . . . . . . .` ✓
- R3: `. . . . . . . .` ✓
- R2: `P P P . . P P P` ✓
- R1: `. . K R . . . .` ✓

Diagram 4 board matches FEN. Legal (one king each, no pawns on edge ranks).

Now the analysis (line 132): "White plays **1.Rd5!**, attacking the bishop. The bishop cannot be defended and must move; White wins it or trades favorably."

Wait — this is a problem. The rook is on d1, bishop on c5. `1.Rd5` puts the rook on d5, attacking the c5-bishop. But the bishop on c5 simply moves away — and `1.Rd5` doesn't win anything. The bishop is NOT pinned and IS defended/can flee. The text claims "The bishop cannot be defended and must move; White wins it or trades favorably" — but the bishop just retreats (e.g., `1...Bb6` or `1...Be7`) and nothing is won. This is **unsound** — there's no pin and no material win.

The section title is "Example 2 — The absolute pin freezes a defender" but the position contains no pin whatsoever. The text even resorts to "imagine the king behind the c5-bishop... a White bishop on b6 would freeze it completely" — i.e., admitting the position doesn't actually show a pin.

Both Diagram 2 and Example 2 are unsound (no actual pin, no actual win). Let me also check the exercises and their answers.

**Exercise 1 (line 151):** FEN `r1bqk2r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/2b1p1B1/4P3/2N5/PPPP1PPP/R2QKBNR w KQkq - 0 1` — answer claims `1.Nd5!` piles on the pinned f6-knight.

input.command:
echo "Exercise 1 FEN: r1bqk2r/pppp1ppp/2n2n2/2b1p1B1/4P3/2N5/PPPP1PPP/R2QKBNR w KQkq - 0 1"
echo "R8 r1bqk2r = r . b q k . . r"
echo "R7 pppp1ppp= p p p p . p p p"
echo "R6 2n2n2   = . . n . . n . ."
echo "R5 2b1p1B1 = . . b . p . B ."
echo "R4 4P3     = . . . . P . . ."
echo "R3 2N5     = . . N . . . . ."
echo "R2 PPPP1PPP= P P P P . P P P"
echo "R1 R2QKBNR = R . . Q K B N R"
echo ""
echo "Bg5 pins Nf6 to Qd8. 1.Nd5 (Nc3-d5) attacks pinned f6 knight 2nd time."
echo "Nd5 attacks f6, c7, e7, b6, b4, etc. Can Nf6xd5? relative pin -> 1...Nxd5 2.Bxd8 wins Q (Bg5xd8)."
echo "Is Nc3 free to go d5? c3 to d5 is a knight move (c3->d5 yes). d5 empty. Legal."
echo "But wait: is White king safe? After 1.Nd5 nothing checks White. After 1...Nxd5 2.Bxd8 Kxd8 (king on e8 can take d8) - White wins Q for N. WINNING."
echo "1...Nxe4? (the OTHER knight pin escape) Nf6 is pinned, can't... Actually 1.Nd5 - does Black have 1...Bxf2+!? Bc5xf2+ Kxf2 Nxe4+ forking? Let's check: 1.Nd5 Bxf2+ 2.Kxf2 Nxe4+ (Nf6xe4+, knight was pinned but now it's a check that's discovered? No - moving Nf6 exposes Qd8 to Bg5. 2...Nxe4+ is check to Kf2, but it ILLEGALLY leaves Qd8 hanging? It's legal (relative pin) but 3.Kxe4?? no. Actually after 2...Nxe4+ 3.Ke1 (or Kg1) and now Bg5 still eyes d8? Bg5xd8 wins Q next. Black has B+N for... hmm messy."
echo "The MAIN line 1.Nd5 Nxd5 2.Bxd8 is clearly winning. Theme sound."

content.text:
Exercise 1 is fine — `1.Nd5!` piling on the pinned knight with `1...Nxd5 2.Bxd8` is the sound main theme. The board would match the FEN (it's an exercise FEN, no ASCII board to check). Exercise 1 is good.

**Exercise 2 (line 152):** FEN `5rk1/ppp2ppp/5n2/3B4/8/8/PPP2PPP/2KR4 w - - 0 1` — answer claims `1.Rd5!` attacks the f6-knight which is "tied to the d8-king diagonal idea."

input.command:
echo "Exercise 2 FEN: 5rk1/ppp2ppp/5n2/3B4/8/8/PPP2PPP/2KR4 w - - 0 1"
echo "R8 5rk1   = . . . . . r k ."
echo "R7 ppp2ppp= p p p . . p p p"
echo "R6 5n2    = . . . . . n . ."
echo "R5 3B4    = . . . B . . . ."
echo "R4 8      = . . . . . . . ."
echo "R3 8      = . . . . . . . ."
echo "R2 PPP2PPP= P P P . . P P P"
echo "R1 2KR4   = . . K R . . . ."
echo ""
echo "White: Kc1, Rd1, Bd5, pawns a2 b2 c2 f2 g2 h2"
echo "Black: Kg8, Rf8, Nf6, pawns a7 b7 c7 f7 g7 h7"
echo ""
echo "Answer says 1.Rd5! attacks the f6-knight. But Rd1-d5? d5 is occupied by WHITE bishop! Rd5 is ILLEGAL/impossible."
echo "Also the knight f6 is NOT tied to any 'd8-king diagonal' - king is on g8, not d8. The answer is nonsense."
echo "And '1...Ne8 2.Re5' - rook can't even get to d5 (own bishop there)."
echo "Bd5 attacks Nf6? d5->f6? No, bishop d5 diagonal: e6,f7,g8(check? Bd5 hits g8 king! that's check on the a2-g8 diagonal: d5-e6-f7? f7 has black pawn. So Bd5 does NOT hit g8 (blocked by f7 pawn). Bd5 hits f7 pawn (defended by king/rook)."
echo "Does Bd5 attack Nf6? No, f6 is not on bishop's diagonal from d5 (d5 diagonals: a2-b3-c4-d5-e6-f7-g8 and g2-f3-e4-d5-c6-b7-a8). f6 not on them."
echo "This exercise is BROKEN: no pin, Rd5 impossible (own piece), king isn't on d-file/diagonal."

content.text:
Exercise 2 is broken: `1.Rd5!` is impossible (d5 is occupied by White's own bishop), there is no pin, and the king isn't on the d-file. The answer is incoherent.

**Exercise 3 (line 153):** FEN `r2qkbnr/ppp2ppp/2np4/4p1b1/4P3/3P1N2/PPP2PPP/RNBQKB1R w KQkq - 0 1` — answer claims `1.h3!` then `2.g4` traps the g5-bishop.

input.command:
echo "Exercise 3 FEN: r2qkbnr/ppp2ppp/2np4/4p1b1/4P3/3P1N2/PPP2PPP/RNBQKB1R w KQkq - 0 1"
echo "R8 r2qkbnr = r . . q k b n r"
echo "R7 ppp2ppp = p p p . . p p p"
echo "R6 2np4    = . . n p . . . ."
echo "R5 4p1b1   = . . . . p . b ."
echo "R4 4P3     = . . . . P . . ."
echo "R3 3P1N2   = . . . P . N . ."
echo "R2 PPP2PPP = P P P . . P P P"
echo "R1 RNBQKB1R= R N B Q K B . R"
echo ""
echo "White: Ke1, Qd1, Ra1,Rh1, Bc1,Bf1, Nb1,Nf3, pawns a2 b2 c2 d3 e4 f2 g2 h2"
echo "Black: Ke8, Qd8, Ra8,Rh8, Bf8,Bg5, Nc6,Ng8, pawns a7 b7 c7 d6 e5 f7 g7 h7"
echo ""
echo "Black bishop on g5. White wants to trap it. 1.h3 (intending g4 then h4/g5 net)."
echo "But Bg5 - is it really trapped after g4? Bg5 retreats: after 1.h3, bishop can go h6,h4,f4,e3,d2(check?),c1,e7,d8,f6..."
echo "Actually 1.h3 threatens 2.g4 and then the bishop's retreat squares on the c1-h6 diagonal: if pawns go h4&g... "
echo "Classic trap: 1.h3 Bh5? no. The bishop on g5 attacked by h-pawn... wait h3 doesn't attack g5. g4 doesn't attack g5 either (pawn g4 attacks f5,h5)."
echo "To attack Bg5 with a pawn you need h4 (h-pawn to h4 attacks g5) or f4 (f-pawn attacks g5)."
echo "1.h3 then 2.g4 does NOT attack the g5 bishop. The bishop sits on g5 happily. 'g4 controls f5 and h5' - irrelevant to g5."
echo "To win Bg5: 1.h4! Bxh4? or 1.h4 attacks g5; bishop must move; if 1...Bf4 2.g3 traps? 1...Bh6 fine. 1...Bxf3? gives up bishop."
echo "Actually the real trap: bishop on g5, play h4 then g3-ish? Let me think: 1.h4 Bf6 (retreats fine) or 1...Bh6 then 2.g4 then 3.g5 traps the h6 bishop? g5 attacks h6! yes. So 1.h4 Bh6 2.g4 (threat g5) ...then g5 forks/traps Bh6. But bishop can go to f8? Bh6 retreat: f8,g7? g7 own pawn. So 1.h4 Bh6 2.g4 and 3.g5 wins bishop IF it can't escape via f8/c1... Bh6 can go to f8 (h6-g7? blocked by g7 pawn -> not via g7; h6-f8 not a move; bishop h6 diagonal: g7(pawn),f8,g5(pawn h4 covers? h4 attacks g5),f4,e3,d2,c1). After 2.g4, bishop h6 can still go f8? h6 to f8 is NOT one diagonal (h6-g7-f8, g7 occupied by black pawn -> blocked!). So Bh6 trapped after pawns advance? It can go f4-e3-d2-c1 side: h6-g5? g5 attacked by h4. Risky."
echo "The answer's specifics (1.h3 then g4 controlling f5/h5) are WRONG - those squares don't trap a g5 bishop. Theme (trap bishop with pawns) is salvageable but mechanics stated are incorrect."
